text: Welcome to this video from James Cook University Hospital, where I'll be performing a VATS diaphragm placation on the left side.

I then have pressurized the cavity at 12 millimeters of mercury.

I've then been able to place my two ports just over the diaphragm and I'm now looking at the laxity of the diaphragm.

I'm very pleased to use the EndoStitch.

I like to placate from anteromedial along to postural lateral on the diaphragm.
And I know there are lots of different ways to do it.

plenty of different directions but this is the way that makes most sense to myself so I'm forming placation stitches I have tried to add a pledget either side although they are quite thin and quite a few of them did fall off initially although the majority have successfully got pledgets on
So I perform a U-stitch, interrupted U-stitches sequentially.
I place a pledget on the suturing side and we're using a knot pusher to tighten the knot from externally.

Certainly the plication distance looks quite far initially but that's why at the start it's important to size up how big your plication is going to be so you don't have to redo any because of over laxity.

There's also some description in the literature of being too tight, although obviously the aim of this operation with a paralysed phrenic nerve is to create a flat, splinted diaphragm to increase lung volume.
Obviously the phrenic nerve will never actually come back.

The anterior portion and now the posterior portion.

For this video I was bringing the second pledget into the operative field and placing the pledget inside the chest, although alternatively I could have

withdrawn the endo stitch and placed the second pledget outside the chest also although I thought this maybe would make it less likely for it to get caught on the port as there is a flap valve at the very top of the CO2 port so we'll be sequentially moving along the diaphragm
performing interrupted U-stitches which are pledgeted either side.
There are many descriptions in the literature.
There are plenty of descriptions of figure-of-eight sutures or U-sutures without pledges.
There are descriptions of plagiatid sutures and then a pericardium placed over the top and sutured with a continuous suture.

There's even a few descriptions using a stapler to lift up the diaphragm and staple across the diaphragm although there are also quite a few case reports where that fails and you get quite significant
diaphragmatic rupture and bowel contents in the chest.

You've actually got to be quite careful, perhaps slightly bigger pledgets than my early ones.
Alternatively, just bring the endostitch out of the chest and put your pledgets on outside the chest.

we're approaching halfway and you can also see very nicely the invagination of the redundant tissue under the thoracic portion of the diaphragm there I've elected to start posteriorly first and then move to anteriorly

Some of the critics of VATS diaphragm placation would cite that you may not be sure that you're not getting abdominal contents but I'm sure you can see from this video we're lifting up the very floppy diaphragm at every stage and to my mind there's no chance of

accidentally hitting spleen liver or abdominal contents there's clearly nothing in that fold there whatsoever and equally posteriorly if you clip nice and firmly you can see a nice fold of diaphragm so we're clearly not stapling any abdominal contents and also the co2 allows this floppiness if you didn't have co2
then perhaps in an open you do need to use your fingers to pull it together.
The other criticism of VATS placation would be that you can't adequately assess how tight your diaphragm is going to be.

and also I didn't feel it was necessary to detach the inferior pulmonary ligament although this is described quite a lot in the literature and I pretty much didn't touch the lung at all in fact during this operation therefore I took the drain out at six hours on the same day of surgery as the lung was fully inflated on the chest x-ray

I'd also placed a nasogastric tube in the gentleman's stomach and we'd suction the stomach as air can often get into the stomach on intubation and I've left that NG tube in overnight as occasionally patients can get an ileus or feel just slightly tender or sore in the abdomen due to the new tight diaphragm and displacement of abdominal contents
I used a double lumen tube, although after entry into the test and CO2 insufflation I actually placed the patient on two lungs and the patient remained on two lungs throughout the operation.
